name: Constellation Bar
insider_tip: Friday has a live jazz band — bar seats don't need reservations, but the window seats upstairs fill up fast. Staff speak English and are happy to explain the whisky menu.

name: Shiyili Craft Beer
insider_tip: Wednesday is 'tap change day' — ask the bartender what's new, they always have seasonal limited releases. This is a real locals' bar, almost no expats — bring patience for ordering if your Chinese is limited, but they're friendly.

name: Seesaw Coffee Jing'an
insider_tip: After 3pm it's usually easy to get a seat. The Yirgacheffe pour-over is their signature — just say 'Yirgacheffe' and point, no Chinese needed. Good WiFi, most staff speak basic English.

name: The Rooster
insider_tip: One of the best IPA selections in Shanghai. Weekends get packed — weeknights are better. English menu available, very expat-friendly crowd, good place to meet people.

name: Volar
insider_tip: Saturday has a jazz quartet — book a seat in advance (WeChat or walk-in before 9pm). Two-drink minimum to enter, which is standard for Shanghai jazz venues. Staff speak English well.

name: Hidden Space
insider_tip: No sign outside — that's intentional. Finding it means you're already the right kind of customer. Single malt selection is excellent. This is the Shanghai 'hidden bar' culture: the less visible, the more serious about what they do.

name: Manner Coffee Wukang Road
insider_tip: Bring your own cup for ¥5 off — this is a very Chinese coffee culture habit. Wukang Road is one of Shanghai's most beautiful streets (French Concession era plane trees). This branch is less crowded than the tourist spots nearby.

name: Blue Note Shanghai
insider_tip: The only Blue Note in China — same ownership as the NYC original. Monthly lineup posted on WeChat (search '蓝调上海'), major touring artists always stop here. Book online if you can, walk-ins possible but risky on show nights.

name: Blackbird
insider_tip: The turntable plays real vinyl — that's not decoration. Get friendly with the bartender and they'll recommend off-menu picks. Very mixed crowd of locals and expats, easy to strike up conversations.

name: Café del Volcan
insider_tip: Owner is Colombian, beans sourced directly from farms. Weekend cuppings are free to join — just show up. One of the few places in Shanghai where you can have a real conversation about coffee origins in English.

name: The Cannery
insider_tip: Bartender trained at The NoMad — the kimchi cocktail is a signature and not on the menu, just ask. Full English menu, English-speaking staff. This is the kind of bar that feels international but isn't a tourist trap.

name: Bitter & Twisted
insider_tip: No large group reservations — they keep it intimate on purpose. Order a Bitters Old Fashioned: it's an unspoken test of taste that the regulars all know about. Staff speak English, very welcoming to solo visitors.

name: Sparrow House (麻雀瓦舍)
insider_tip: Unannounced intimate shows happen here — follow their WeChat account (search '麻雀瓦舍') to find out 3 days before ticket platforms. This is Chinese indie folk scene: acoustic, emotional, very different from what you'd expect.

name: % Arabica Bund
insider_tip: Weekday afternoons before 2pm are quieter. The Bund-view seat is genuinely worth it — you're looking at Pudong's skyline across the river. Latte here is slightly stronger than other branches. English menu, tourist-friendly.

name: Wooden Box
insider_tip: Yangpu is the university district — very few tourists make it here, which is exactly why locals love it. Wednesday ladies' night has good deals. Owner has deep craft beer knowledge, will chat in basic English if you show interest.

name: Brownstone
insider_tip: Less frequent shows than Blue Note, but the audience genuinely comes for the music — no one's there just to be seen. More private atmosphere. This is what Shanghai's underground jazz scene actually looks like.

name: REX Coffee
insider_tip: No WiFi password posted — ask the owner, he'll give it quietly. Very neighborhood feel, mostly local regulars. Music curation is excellent. This is the kind of place that doesn't need to advertise because the right people always find it.
